courseTitle: Awareness of Learning Disability and Autism

items.html:
What a learning disability is

A learning disability is a lifelong condition that affects the way a person understands new or complex information, learns new skills, and copes independently with everyday life. It begins before adulthood and has a lasting effect on development. A person may need extra time, clearer explanations, or practical support to do things that others find straightforward.

Learning disabilities range from mild to profound. Some people you support will live largely independently with a little help; others will need support with almost every part of daily life. Each person is an individual with their own strengths, preferences, relationships, and goals.

  • Not an illness: a learning disability is not something to be cured or treated away.
  • Not a mental illness: though a person can also experience mental ill health, as anyone can.
  • Lifelong: it is a difference in how someone learns and manages daily living, not a phase.

What it is not — and a note on this course

A learning disability is different from a learning difficulty such as dyslexia, which affects specific skills but not general everyday functioning. It is also different from acquired brain injury or dementia, which develop later in life.

Important compliance statement: This e-learning gives you the knowledge and awareness element only. It is not the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training on Learning Disability and Autism and does not replace it. Under the Health and Care Act 2022, your employer must ensure you complete the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training at the tier appropriate to your role, delivered by an approved provider and co-delivered with people with lived experience of learning disability and autism.

  • Awareness level: this course builds foundational understanding.
  • Your duty: apply respect and reasonable adjustments in daily practice.

Communicating well

Good communication is the foundation of good care. People with a learning disability communicate in many ways — through speech, but also through pictures, symbols, objects, signing such as Makaton, body language, and behaviour. Never assume that someone who does not use words has nothing to say.

Simple habits make a real difference:

  • Slow down: give the person time to take in what you say and to respond.
  • Keep it clear: use short sentences, plain words, and one idea at a time.
  • Use easy-read: pictures alongside simple text support understanding.
  • Check, do not assume: ask the person to show you or tell you back, rather than assuming they have understood.

Diagnostic overshadowing

Diagnostic overshadowing happens when a person's physical or mental health symptoms are wrongly put down to their learning disability, so a real, treatable illness is missed. For example, a change in behaviour might be a sign of pain, infection, constipation, or a dental problem — not simply “how they are”.

This is one of the most serious risks in health and social care, because it delays diagnosis and treatment. You can help prevent it:

  • Notice change: know the person's baseline, and take any change seriously.
  • Look for causes: distress or withdrawal often signals an unmet physical need.
  • Speak up: report and record what you see, and push for it to be investigated.
  • Advocate: stay alongside the person during health appointments.

items.question: A person you support who is usually cheerful becomes withdrawn and hits out at mealtimes. What is the best first response?

answers.text: Consider that a physical cause such as toothache or pain may be behind the change, and report it to be checked.
answers.feedback: Correct. A change in behaviour often signals an unmet physical need. Investigating rather than assuming helps prevent diagnostic overshadowing.

answers.text: Assume this is just part of their learning disability and carry on as normal.
answers.feedback: This is diagnostic overshadowing — putting symptoms down to the learning disability, so a treatable cause could be missed.

answers.text: Stop offering meals until the behaviour settles.
answers.feedback: This risks the person's nutrition and ignores the possible cause. The change needs to be understood, not avoided.

answers.text: Record it only if it happens several more times.
answers.feedback: Delaying reporting can delay treatment. A clear change from baseline should be reported and recorded promptly.

items.html:
Health inequalities

People with a learning disability face significant health inequalities. On average they experience poorer health and die younger than the general population, often from conditions that could have been prevented or treated. These deaths are frequently linked to barriers in care rather than to the learning disability itself.

Barriers include:

  • Access: services that are hard to reach, book, or understand.
  • Communication: information not provided in accessible formats.
  • Overshadowing: symptoms wrongly attributed to the learning disability.
  • Attitudes: assumptions about someone's quality of life affecting decisions.

Recognising that these deaths are often avoidable is the first step. Your everyday vigilance and advocacy directly help to close this gap.

Learning from lives and deaths — LeDeR

The LeDeR programme (Learning from Lives and Deaths — People with a Learning Disability and Autistic People) reviews the deaths of people with a learning disability, and of autistic people, in England. Its purpose is not to blame, but to learn.

By looking at what happened, LeDeR identifies patterns and common problems — such as delayed diagnosis or missed reasonable adjustments — and turns them into improvements in care.

  • Aim: to reduce avoidable deaths and health inequalities.
  • How: structured, respectful reviews that involve families and carers.
  • Your part: good records and honest reporting feed this learning.

At awareness level, you simply need to know that LeDeR exists, why it matters, and that the quality of your daily recording supports it.

items.question: The main purpose of the LeDeR programme is to blame individual staff for the deaths of people with a learning disability.
items.correctMsg: Correct. LeDeR is about learning, not blame. It reviews deaths to spot patterns and improve care so that avoidable deaths are reduced.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. LeDeR is a learning programme, not a blame process. It reviews lives and deaths to identify improvements in care.

items.html:
What autism is

Autism is a lifelong difference in how a person experiences and makes sense of the world. It affects how someone communicates, relates to other people, and processes information and their surroundings. Autism is not an illness or a disease, and it cannot, and should not, be “cured”.

Autism is often described as a spectrum, but this does not mean a simple line from “a little” to “a lot”. Each autistic person has a unique profile of strengths and challenges that can vary from day to day and from one situation to another.

  • Communication: differences in understanding and using language, tone, and social cues.
  • Interests and routine: deep, focused interests and comfort in predictability.
  • Strengths: honesty, attention to detail, loyalty, and unique perspectives.

Not a learning disability — and sensory differences

Autism is not a learning disability. Many autistic people do not have a learning disability at all. However, the two can co-occur, and a person may have both. Always find out about the individual rather than assuming.

Many autistic people experience sensory differences: the world can feel too loud, too bright, or overwhelming. Some senses may be heightened (hypersensitive) and others reduced (hyposensitive).

  • Sound: everyday noise can feel painful or exhausting.
  • Light and touch: lighting, textures, or labels in clothing may cause real distress.
  • Environment: small adjustments — a quieter space, dimmer lights — can transform someone's experience.

Many autistic people prefer identity-first language (“autistic person”) rather than person-first (“person with autism”). Ask each person what they prefer.

items.question: Which statement about autism and learning disability is correct?

answers.text: Autism is not a learning disability, but a person can have both.
answers.feedback: Correct. Autism and learning disability are different things. They sometimes co-occur, so always find out about the individual.

answers.text: Autism and learning disability are the same thing.
answers.feedback: They are different. Autism is a difference in how someone experiences the world; a learning disability affects learning and everyday functioning.

answers.text: Every autistic person also has a learning disability.
answers.feedback: Many autistic people do not have a learning disability. Assuming they do is inaccurate and unhelpful.

answers.text: A person can never have both autism and a learning disability.
answers.feedback: They can co-occur. Some people you support will have both, each affecting them in individual ways.

items.html:
Communication and processing information

Autistic people often communicate and process information differently. Language may be understood very literally, so sarcasm, hints, and figures of speech can be confusing. Facial expressions and tone may not carry the meaning you expect, in either direction.

Processing spoken information can also take longer. What looks like ignoring you may simply be someone still working out what you said. You can help by:

  • Being literal and clear: say exactly what you mean, avoiding idioms.
  • Allowing processing time: pause after speaking and wait patiently.
  • Reducing demands: ask one thing at a time, and back words up with writing or pictures.
  • Respecting communication style: some people prefer text, email, or gestures to speech.

Masking

Masking is when an autistic person hides or suppresses their natural responses in order to appear to fit in — for example forcing eye contact, copying others' behaviour, or holding in distress. It is often unconscious and can be a way of staying safe or accepted.

Masking is exhausting. It can hide how much support a person actually needs, and the effort may lead to burnout, anxiety, or a delayed “crash” once the person is somewhere they feel safe.

  • Do not be misled: someone coping on the surface may be struggling underneath.
  • Create safety: a person who feels accepted has less need to mask.
  • Believe people: take reported difficulties seriously even if they are not visible.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the passage about masking.
items.textField: Masking is when an autistic person *hides* their natural responses to appear to fit in. It is often *exhausting* and can conceal how much *support* someone truly needs. A person who feels *accepted* has less need to mask.

items.html:
Meltdown and shutdown

When an autistic person is completely overwhelmed — often by sensory overload, too many demands, or unexpected change — they may experience a meltdown or a shutdown. Neither is bad behaviour, naughtiness, or something done deliberately. Both are responses to distress that the person cannot control.

  • Meltdown: an intense, outward response — the person may shout, cry, or move suddenly as overwhelming feelings spill over.
  • Shutdown: an inward response — the person may go quiet, still, or withdrawn, becoming unable to respond or communicate.

A shutdown can be easy to miss because it is quiet, but the person is just as overwhelmed as during a meltdown.

What helps

Your calm, low-key response matters enormously. The goal is to reduce demands and help the person feel safe, not to talk them out of how they feel.

  • Reduce input: lower noise and light, and give space and quiet.
  • Stay calm: keep your voice gentle and your language minimal.
  • Do not crowd: avoid too many questions, instructions, or touch.
  • Give time: allow the person to recover at their own pace.
  • Learn triggers: knowing what overwhelms someone helps you prevent it next time.

Afterwards, a person may be tired and need rest. Recording what led up to it, respectfully, helps everyone understand and reduce future distress.

items.question: An autistic person you support suddenly goes silent, still, and stops responding in a busy, noisy room. What is happening, and what helps most?

answers.text: This may be a shutdown; reduce noise and light, give space and quiet, and allow time to recover.
answers.feedback: Correct. A quiet, withdrawn response can be a shutdown. Lowering demands and sensory input, and allowing recovery time, is what helps.

answers.text: They are being rude and ignoring you, so you should insist they answer.
answers.feedback: A shutdown is not rudeness or a choice. Insisting on answers adds demand and makes overwhelm worse.

answers.text: Nothing is wrong because they are quiet, so no support is needed.
answers.feedback: A shutdown is easy to miss precisely because it is quiet, but the person is overwhelmed and does need calm support.

answers.text: Move closer, ask lots of questions, and touch their arm to get a response.
answers.feedback: Crowding, questioning, and touch increase sensory input and demand, which can deepen the shutdown.

items.html:
The duty to make reasonable adjustments

Under the Equality Act 2010, disability is a protected characteristic, and organisations have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments so that disabled people are not put at a disadvantage. This applies across health and social care, and it is anticipatory: services should plan ahead rather than wait for a problem.

A reasonable adjustment is a change that removes or reduces a barrier. It does not mean giving someone an unfair advantage — it means giving them fair access to the same care and opportunities as everyone else.

  • Legal, not optional: failing to adjust can be unlawful discrimination.
  • Individual: what is reasonable depends on the person and the situation.
  • Everyday: many adjustments are small, cost little, and take moments.

Adjustments in practice

Reasonable adjustments are practical and personal. The key is to ask the individual what helps them, and then to make it happen.

  • Communication: easy-read information, extra time, pictures, or a communication aid.
  • Environment: a quieter room, dimmed lights, or the first or last appointment of the day.
  • Time: longer appointments so that nothing is rushed.
  • Familiarity: the same worker where possible, and a trusted person present.
  • Preparation: a visit in advance so a place or procedure feels less frightening.

Recording someone's adjustments clearly, so every worker knows them, means the person does not have to explain their needs over and over. Consistency is part of respect.

items.question: Making reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010 is a legal duty, not just a nice thing to do when there is time.
items.correctMsg: Correct. The duty to make reasonable adjustments is a legal requirement, and failing to make them can amount to unlawful discrimination.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Reasonable adjustments are a legal duty under the Equality Act 2010, not an optional extra.

items.html:
Hospital and health passports

A hospital passport (also called a health passport) is a personal document that travels with a person into a healthcare setting. It gives staff who do not know them the essential information they need to provide safe, personalised care — quickly, and without the person having to repeat it while unwell or anxious.

A passport typically includes:

  • How I communicate: the person's words, signs, and cues.
  • What matters to me: likes, dislikes, and how I show pain or distress.
  • My health: conditions, medicines, and allergies.
  • My reasonable adjustments: what I need to feel safe and understood.

You can help by keeping a person's passport up to date and making sure it goes with them.

Annual health checks

People with a learning disability aged 14 and over are entitled to a free annual health check from their GP, even if they feel well. Because health problems in this group are so often missed, these checks are a vital way of finding and treating issues early — directly tackling health inequalities.

The check reviews the person's overall health, medicines, and any changes, and produces a health action plan for the year ahead.

  • Encourage: support the person to attend and to prepare.
  • Register: the person needs to be on the GP's learning disability register.
  • Follow up: help make sure actions from the plan actually happen.

Your encouragement and practical support can make the difference between a check happening or being missed.

items.question: What is the main purpose of a hospital (health) passport?

answers.text: To give healthcare staff who do not know the person the key information they need to provide safe, personalised care.
answers.feedback: Correct. A hospital passport shares how the person communicates, what matters to them, and their health needs and adjustments, so care is safe and personal.

answers.text: To replace the need for the person to attend appointments.
answers.feedback: A passport supports appointments; it does not replace them. The person still receives care in person.

answers.text: To act as legal proof of the person's diagnosis for benefits.
answers.feedback: A passport is a care tool for sharing needs and preferences, not a legal or benefits document.

answers.text: To record staff rotas and shift patterns on the ward.
answers.feedback: A passport is about the individual person's needs, not staff organisation.

items.html:
Over-medication and STOMP

For many years, people with a learning disability, autistic people, or both have been given psychotropic medicines — drugs that affect the mind, such as antipsychotics — when they do not have the mental health condition those drugs are meant to treat. Too often the medicines were used to manage behaviour, sometimes for years, when other support would have been better.

These medicines can cause real harm: side effects, sedation, weight gain, and serious long-term health risks. STOMP stands for Stopping Over-Medication of People with a learning disability, autism or both. It is a national project working to make sure medicines are used safely, appropriately, and only when genuinely needed.

  • Right reason: medicine should treat a real condition, not simply control behaviour.
  • Right review: prescriptions should be checked regularly.

Your part in STOMP

You are not a prescriber, but you are often the person who knows the individual best, so your observations matter. STOMP works alongside STAMP, which promotes the same safe approach for children and young people.

  • Understand behaviour: distress usually has a cause — pain, fear, an unmet need — not simply something to medicate.
  • Support alternatives: good communication, routine, activity, and reasonable adjustments can reduce distress.
  • Observe and record: note effects and side effects of medicines and share them with the team.
  • Support reviews: help make sure medicines are reviewed and questioned, not just repeated.

The aim is never simply to stop all medicines, but to make sure each person is on the right medicine, at the right dose, for the right reason.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the summary of STOMP.
items.textField: STOMP means Stopping Over-Medication of People with a learning disability, autism or both. It aims to reduce the inappropriate use of *psychotropic* medicines that were often used to manage *behaviour* rather than to treat a real condition. These medicines should be *reviewed* regularly, and other support such as good *communication* should be offered instead.

items.html:
The Health and Care Act 2022

The Health and Care Act 2022 introduced a legal requirement that health and social care staff receive training on learning disability and autism appropriate to their role. This was a direct response to campaigning by families — most notably the family of Oliver McGowan, a young autistic man with a learning disability who died after being given medication against his and his family's wishes.

The result is the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training on Learning Disability and Autism: the government's preferred, standardised training to meet this legal duty. Its aim is to give staff the right knowledge and skills so that people with a learning disability and autistic people receive safe, compassionate, and informed care.

  • Legal basis: mandated under the Health and Care Act 2022.
  • Named after Oliver: created so that his death leads to lasting change.

The two tiers and lived experience

The Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training is delivered at two tiers, and your employer decides which applies to your role.

  • Tier 1: for staff who need general awareness — those in roles with limited or no direct care responsibility.
  • Tier 2: for staff who provide care and support directly, including most care workers, requiring deeper knowledge and skills.

A defining feature is co-delivery with people with lived experience: the training is delivered together with people with a learning disability and autistic people. This ensures learning is grounded in real understanding, not just theory.

Remember: the course you are doing now is awareness only. It does not count as the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training, which your employer must arrange through an approved provider.

items.question: Which statement best describes the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training?

answers.text: It is delivered at two tiers and co-delivered with people who have lived experience of learning disability and autism.
answers.feedback: Correct. The training has Tier 1 and Tier 2, and is co-delivered with people with lived experience, as mandated under the Health and Care Act 2022.

answers.text: It is a single course that everyone completes in exactly the same way.
answers.feedback: It is delivered at two tiers, with the tier matched to the person's role.

answers.text: It is delivered only by academics, without involving people with lived experience.
answers.feedback: Co-delivery with people who have lived experience is a defining feature of the training.

answers.text: This e-learning course counts as the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training.
answers.feedback: It does not. This course is awareness only; your employer must arrange the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training through an approved provider.

items.html:
Capacity and consent

Everyone has the right to make their own decisions wherever they can. Having a learning disability or being autistic does not mean a person lacks the mental capacity to decide. The Mental Capacity Act 2005 sets out clear principles, starting with the assumption that a person has capacity unless it is shown otherwise.

Capacity is decision-specific and can change over time. A person may be able to decide what to eat but need more support with a complex health decision, and that is normal for everyone.

  • Assume capacity: start from the presumption that the person can decide.
  • Unwise is allowed: a decision others think unwise is not proof of lacking capacity.
  • Go deeper: see our Mental Capacity Act course for the full framework.

Supported decision-making

Before anyone concludes that a person cannot make a decision, they must be given all practicable support to make it themselves. This is called supported decision-making, and it is exactly where good communication and reasonable adjustments come together.

  • Accessible information: use easy-read, pictures, objects, or video.
  • Right time and place: choose a calm moment and a comfortable setting.
  • Break it down: present one part of the decision at a time.
  • Involve trusted people: a familiar worker or advocate can help.

If a person is assessed as unable to make a specific decision even with support, any decision made for them must be in their best interests and as least restrictive as possible — never simply what is easiest for others.

items.question: Having a learning disability or being autistic automatically means a person lacks the mental capacity to make their own decisions.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Capacity must never be assumed absent. The Mental Capacity Act starts by presuming capacity, and support must be given before deciding otherwise.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. A learning disability or autism does not remove capacity. Capacity is decision-specific and must be presumed unless shown otherwise.

items.html:
Restrictive practice and least restriction

A restrictive practice is anything that limits a person's movement, freedom, or choices — from physically stopping someone, to locking a door, to withholding something they want, or using medication to control behaviour. Restrictive practices can cause real harm and can breach a person's human rights.

The guiding rule is the least restrictive principle: any restriction must be the minimum necessary, used only to prevent harm, for the shortest time, and always in the person's best interests.

  • Last resort: restriction is never a first response or a routine.
  • Proportionate: it must match the level of real risk.
  • Accountable: it must be recorded, reported, and reviewed.

Positive behaviour support

Positive behaviour support (PBS) is a respectful, person-centred approach that aims to improve someone's quality of life and, in doing so, reduce behaviour that others find challenging. It starts from a simple idea: behaviour is a form of communication, and distress usually has a cause.

  • Understand the why: look for the unmet need behind the behaviour — pain, fear, boredom, or a sensory issue.
  • Change the environment: reduce triggers and add things that help.
  • Build a good life: meaningful activity, relationships, and choice reduce distress.
  • Prevent, do not react: the goal is to stop crises before they start.

At awareness level, remember this: understanding and preventing distress is always better than restricting a person once they are already in crisis.

items.question: Which approach best reflects the least restrictive principle and positive behaviour support?

answers.text: Look for the cause behind a person's distress and change the environment to prevent it, using any restriction only as a proportionate last resort.
answers.feedback: Correct. PBS treats behaviour as communication and focuses on prevention, while any restriction must be minimal, proportionate, and a last resort.

answers.text: Use restriction routinely to keep situations under control.
answers.feedback: Restriction is never routine. It must be the least restrictive option, used only to prevent harm and for the shortest time.

answers.text: Ignore the reasons behind behaviour and focus only on stopping it.
answers.feedback: Behaviour is communication. Ignoring the cause misses the point and does not help the person.

answers.text: Withhold things the person enjoys as a way of managing their behaviour.
answers.feedback: Withholding what someone wants is itself a restrictive practice and can cause harm; it is not positive behaviour support.

items.html:
Respect, adjust, include

You have completed an awareness introduction to learning disability and autism. Three ideas run through everything you have learned:

  • Respect: every person is an individual with their own strengths, preferences, and rights. Learning disability and autism are differences, not deficits, and neither is an illness to be cured.
  • Adjust: make the reasonable adjustments each person needs — in communication, environment, and time — so they get fair access to good care. Watch for diagnostic overshadowing, support annual health checks and hospital passports, and question over-medication through STOMP.
  • Include: presume capacity, support people to make their own decisions, use the least restrictive approach, and understand behaviour as communication.

Small, consistent actions from you help close the health inequalities that people with a learning disability and autistic people still face.

Your legal obligation — compliance statement

Please read this carefully. This e-learning has given you the knowledge and awareness element only. It is not the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training on Learning Disability and Autism, and completing it does not meet that requirement.

Under the Health and Care Act 2022, your employer must ensure you complete the Oliver McGowan Mandatory Training at the tier appropriate to your role — Tier 1 or Tier 2 — delivered by an approved provider and co-delivered with people who have lived experience of learning disability and autism.

  • Next step: speak to your manager to confirm your tier and how your organisation delivers the training.
  • Keep learning: revisit our Mental Capacity Act course to deepen your understanding of consent and best interests.

Thank you for taking the time to learn how to care well for people with a learning disability and autistic people.
